synopsis: Harvard symbologist Robert Langdon is drawn into a murder investigation at the Louvre, where the victim's body is arranged alongside cryptic symbols pointing to a centuries-old secret society. Racing through Paris and beyond with French cryptologist Sophie Neveu, Langdon must decode clues hidden within Leonardo da Vinci's artworks before a shadowy adversary can suppress a truth concealed for generations.

sources.title: The Da Vinci Code - Penguin Random House

synopsis: David Graeber and David Wengrow challenge the standard narrative that human societies progressed inevitably from primitive equality to complex hierarchy, drawing on archaeology and anthropology to show that prehistoric peoples experimented with diverse political arrangements. The book argues that our assumptions about the origins of civilization are largely wrong, and that human history offers far more varied examples of social organization than conventional theories allow.

sources.title: The Dawn of Everything - David Graeber

synopsis: After a meteor shower blinds most of the world's population overnight, engineer Bill Masen awakens in a London hospital to find civilization collapsing around him and predatory mobile plants called triffids taking advantage of the chaos. The novel follows Masen's efforts to survive and find other sighted survivors as society disintegrates and the triffids begin to dominate.

sources.title: The Day of the Triffids - Penguin Random House

synopsis: Ross Douthat argues that Western societies have entered a period of stagnation characterized by technological plateau, demographic decline, cultural repetition, and political gridlock — a condition he terms decadence. The book examines how prosperity and success have paradoxically produced a civilization that recycles rather than innovates, and considers what forces might eventually break the stalemate.

Donald Norman examines why everyday objects so often frustrate their users, arguing that failures of design rather than failures of human understanding are the real culprit when things go wrong. Using principles drawn from cognitive psychology, the book outlines how good design communicates affordances and constraints so that objects guide users naturally toward correct use.

sources.title: The Design of Everyday Things - Don Norman's JND.org

synopsis: Set in a nanotechnology-enabled future where nation-states have given way to cultural enclaves, the novel follows an illicit interactive primer that falls into the hands of a young girl named Nell growing up in poverty. The book traces how the primer — designed to raise an independent thinker — shapes Nell amid the social upheavals of a neo-Victorian society built on molecular manufacturing.

Anne Frank kept this diary during the two years she and her family spent hiding in a concealed Amsterdam apartment while the Nazis occupied the Netherlands, recording her observations on family tensions, adolescent life, and the world outside. The entries run from June 1942 until August 1944, when the family's hiding place was betrayed and they were deported.

sources.title: The Diary of a Young Girl - Penguin Random House

synopsis: Seth Godin introduces the concept of the Dip — the difficult stretch that separates early enthusiasm from genuine mastery in any endeavor — and argues that knowing when to push through versus when to quit is the key strategic skill. The book makes the case that strategic quitting frees resources for the pursuits where one can realistically become the best in the world.

Physicist Shevek, raised on the anarchist moon Anarres, travels to the capitalist planet Urras in hopes of building scientific and human bridges between two worlds that have been isolated from each other for generations. The novel uses the alternating histories of both societies to explore the tensions between individual freedom and collective obligation, property and poverty.

sources.title: The Dispossessed - Ursula K. Le Guin official site

synopsis: Eswar Prasad examines how the 2008 financial crisis paradoxically tightened the U.S. dollar's grip on global finance, tracing structural forces that trap other nations into dependence on dollar assets. The book surveys currency conflicts, U.S.-China economic tensions, and IMF dynamics while arguing that displacing the dollar as the world's reserve currency remains far harder than commonly assumed.

sources.title: The Dollar Trap - Princeton University Press

synopsis: Engineer Dan Davis is drugged and swindled out of his robotics company by his business partners, then uses suspended animation to escape to the future only to find his stolen patents produced exactly as he designed them. The novel combines time travel with a puzzle about how Davis can reach back through time to recover what was taken from him.

M. Mitchell Waldrop traces the life of J.C.R. Licklider, the psychologist-turned-computer-scientist whose vision of interactive human-computer communication laid the conceptual groundwork for both personal computing and the internet. The biography shows how Licklider used his position at ARPA to fund and inspire a generation of researchers who built the systems he had imagined.

Michael Gerber argues that most small businesses fail because their founders — skilled tradespeople, technicians, or professionals — mistake technical expertise for business acumen and become trapped working in the business rather than on it. Drawing on the franchise model as a template, the book prescribes building systematic, replicable processes that allow a business to run without depending entirely on its owner.

Terence Kealey marshals historical evidence from multiple countries to argue that government funding of basic science does not drive economic growth, and that the free market has consistently proven more effective at generating productive research. The book challenges the postwar consensus that public investment in science is necessary for technological advance, proposing instead that industry-led research better matches scientific effort to useful ends.

sources.title: The Economic Laws of Scientific Research - Springer

synopsis: Jonathan Birch investigates how to reason responsibly about beings — animals, human patients with altered consciousness, fetuses, neural organoids, and AI systems — whose capacity for sentience is genuinely uncertain. The book develops a precautionary framework for policy and science that takes seriously the moral risks of being wrong about which entities can suffer.

sources.title: The Edge of Sentience - Oxford University Press

synopsis: Peter Drucker defines effectiveness as consistently doing the right things and argues it is a learnable discipline rather than an innate talent, built from five core practices: managing time, choosing where to contribute, building on strengths, setting priorities, and making sound decisions. The book is addressed to knowledge workers at all levels who must convert their time and thinking into organizational results.

Horace Freeland Judson chronicles the birth of molecular biology from the 1940s through the early 1970s, drawing on interviews with more than one hundred scientists to reconstruct the race to discover the structure of DNA, crack the genetic code, and understand gene regulation. The book renders the science through the voices and rivalries of the researchers themselves, from Watson and Crick to Monod and Jacob.

sources.title: The Eighth Day of Creation - Amazon

synopsis: David Abraham traces the global supply chains of rare metals — elements central to smartphones, weapons systems, and renewable energy technologies — from remote mines to finished products, revealing how a handful of countries and companies control materials that underpin modern civilization. The book examines the geopolitical and environmental consequences of growing dependence on these scarce resources.

sources.title: The Elements of Power - Yale University Press

synopsis: Joël Glenn Brenner spent years gaining unprecedented access to the secretive Hershey and Mars companies, producing a dual portrait of the rival chocolate empires and the contrasting personalities who built them — utopian philanthropist Milton Hershey and the relentlessly competitive Forrest Mars. The book examines the marketing battles, corporate intrigue, and industry dynamics that shaped the global candy business.

sources.title: The Emperors of Chocolate - Publishers Weekly
